resume:
Gregory Pinheiro
Senior Enterprise Account Executive — Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Software
Austin, TX | gpinheiro.sales@example.com | 11 years in B2B technology sales

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Senior enterprise seller focused on net-new logo acquisition and large multi-year expansion in the security and observability space. I carry quota against Fortune 1000 accounts, run complex multi-stakeholder cycles that routinely involve security architects, procurement, and CISO-level economic buyers, and I partner closely with solutions engineering and channel to land and expand. Over my career I have sold endpoint security, SIEM, and cloud workload protection, and I am comfortable navigating both direct and partner-led motions. I am known internally for forecast discipline, for keeping deals honest in the pipeline review, and for being the person leadership trusts to take over a stalled strategic account and get it moving again.

EXPERIENCE

SentinelGrid Security — Senior Enterprise Account Executive (2020 to present), Austin, TX
I own a named-account territory of roughly forty Fortune-1000 logos across the central US, selling our cloud workload protection platform and our managed detection and response offering. My annual quota has ranged from 1.6M to 2.1M in new ARR depending on the fiscal year. I closed at 112 percent of plan in my first full year, 96 percent the following year during a budget-freeze environment, and I was tracking around 130 percent in the most recent year before this search. My average deal size sits between 180K and 320K in annual contract value, and my typical enterprise cycle runs six to nine months from first qualified meeting to signature. I built the territory plan from a cold start when two of the prior reps left, rebuilt relationships with three regional channel partners, and personally drove the two largest deals the central region booked in the last two years — a 1.1M three-year expansion at a national logistics carrier and an 840K new-logo win at a regional hospital network. I run a disciplined MEDDICC qualification on every six-figure opportunity, and I am the rep our RVP usually assigns when a strategic account has gone dark and needs a reset. I also mentor two junior AEs on discovery technique and multithreading, and I have presented our regional win-room methodology at the last two national sales kickoffs.

Key wins and activities at SentinelGrid:
- Closed a 1.1M three-year managed-detection expansion at a national logistics carrier; displaced an incumbent SIEM by building a joint business case with the customer's security operations lead and their CFO's office.
- Landed an 840K new-logo deal at a regional hospital network after an eleven-month cycle that survived two reorganizations on the buyer side.
- Reopened a strategic manufacturing account that had been written off for three quarters and turned it into a 400K pilot that is now expanding.
- Maintained a personal pipeline-to-quota coverage ratio of roughly 3.5x and a forecast accuracy that my RVP cited as the most reliable on the central team.
- Built and ran a quarterly win-room cadence for the central region: I pulled the SE lead, the channel manager, and the customer's project sponsor into a single mutual-close-plan review every two weeks on any deal above 250K, which is the practice I later presented at sales kickoff.
- Coached the two junior AEs on my pod through their first six-figure closes; both finished above plan in the year I mentored them, and one was promoted into a named-account seat.
- Standardized a discovery framework around business outcomes rather than feature checklists, mapping every opportunity to a quantified pain (downtime cost, mean-time-to-detect, audit-finding exposure) before we ever brought in an SE for a technical deep-dive.

CloudHarbor Observability — Enterprise Account Executive (2016 to 2020), Dallas, TX
Sold an APM and log-analytics platform into mid-market and lower-enterprise accounts. Carried a quota that grew from 900K to 1.4M over four years. Finished above plan in three of four years, and was promoted from commercial AE to enterprise AE in year two. I built a repeatable land-and-expand motion around a free-trial entry point, working with our product-led-growth team to identify high-usage trials and convert them into paid enterprise agreements. My largest single deal there was a 620K platform standardization across a retail customer's engineering org. I was also part of the early-adopter group that piloted our move from a seat-based to a consumption-based pricing model, and I gave the field a lot of the early objection-handling language for that transition.

Meridian Business Systems — Account Executive, SMB then Commercial (2013 to 2016), Dallas, TX
Started my software sales career here selling an on-premise IT asset management product to small and mid-sized businesses. Began in a high-velocity SMB role making sixty-plus dials a day, then earned a move to the commercial segment. Consistently hit between 95 and 120 percent of a transactional monthly quota, and won the regional rookie-of-the-year award in my first full year. This is where I learned pipeline hygiene, the discipline of logging everything in the CRM, and how to run a tight qualification conversation under time pressure.

SELECTED SKILLS AND METHODS
MEDDICC and Command-of-the-Message qualification; multi-stakeholder enterprise cycles; security and observability domain fluency (SIEM, EDR, CWPP, APM); channel and partner co-selling; consumption and seat-based commercial models; mutual close plans; executive business-case development; Salesforce and Clari forecasting; territory planning from cold start.

EDUCATION
Bachelor of Business Administration, Marketing — University of Texas at Arlington (2012)

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
A senior or strategic enterprise seat where I can own a small number of large, complex accounts, work a true land-and-expand motion, and partner with a serious solutions-engineering bench. I do my best work when I am trusted to quarterback an account team rather than just carry an activity number.
jd: Strategic Enterprise Account Executive (Security) — own twenty named Fortune-500 accounts for a fast-growing cloud security platform. You will run complex, multi-stakeholder, multi-year cycles from first meeting through procurement and renewal, quarterbacking a virtual account team of solutions engineers, channel partners, and customer-success. Quota is 2.4M new and expansion ARR with an expected average deal size north of 250K ACV and cycles of six to twelve months. We expect rigorous MEDDICC qualification, a documented mutual close plan on every six-figure opportunity, and honest, defensible forecasting in weekly commit reviews. The ideal candidate has personally closed multiple seven-figure multi-year deals, has displaced an entrenched incumbent at least once, and can clearly reconstruct the timeline and sequence of a complex win — who they engaged, in what order, and why. You will report to the RVP, Central Enterprise, and collaborate with product and pricing on consumption-based commercial structures. Travel ~40 percent.

history.q: Take me through your single most complex enterprise win in the last two years — that 1.1M logistics expansion. Reconstruct the timeline for me: who you engaged first, what happened in what order, and how the deal actually progressed from first meeting to signature.

candidate_last_answer: Right, the logistics deal. So we signed the three-year in Q3, and that came after we'd already run the production rollout, which is when the SecOps lead really became my champion. Before that — actually the CFO's office got involved pretty early, I'd say around month two, right after the technical win. Well, the technical win was later, that was after procurement had already started redlining. The first meeting was a partner intro, that was definitely the kickoff, and the pilot ran for about six months, though we also expanded the pilot scope twice before the original pilot even closed. So by the time legal came in, the champion had been bought in since basically the beginning, even though the SecOps lead came around after rollout.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the technical win was later, that was after procurement had already started redlining

expected-yield: a single consistent chronological sequence of the named milestones (partner intro, pilot, technical win, CFO involvement, procurement redlines, SecOps champion, signature) with the months they occurred, resolving whether the technical win came before or after procurement started
